lessons.title: Dealer Upcard Priority
lessons.content: In blackjack, the dealer upcard tells you how aggressive you should be. Weak dealer cards favor patience; strong dealer cards demand tighter choices.

lessons.title: Soft Hand Aggression
lessons.content: Soft hands can absorb a hit without busting, which gives them more flexibility than hard totals in similar spots.

questions.title: Hard 12 versus dealer 4
questions.scenario: You have a hard 12 against a dealer 4.
questions.explanation: A dealer 4 is a weak upcard, so standing on hard 12 is the disciplined default because the dealer is more likely to bust.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: The dealer is in a poor position to build a strong hand.

choices.label: Hit immediately
choices.explanation: You improve too slowly compared with the dealer bust risk.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: This spot does not give enough upside for a double.

questions.title: Soft 18 versus dealer 9
questions.scenario: You have a soft 18 against a dealer 9.
questions.explanation: Soft 18 looks strong, but against a 9 it often needs more improvement because the dealer's upcard is not weak enough to stand comfortably.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Soft totals can take another card without busting, which increases your equity here.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing gives away too much equity against the dealer 9.

choices.label: Surrender
choices.explanation: Surrender is not the best default in this simplified training spot.

lessons.title: Stiff Hands and Patience
lessons.content: Hard 12 through 16 are fragile hands. Against weak dealer cards, you often protect them by standing rather than forcing improvement.

lessons.title: Strong Totals and Pressure
lessons.content: Hard 17 and above are usually made hands. Hard 9 to 11 are often aggressive hands because they can gain value by doubling.

lessons.title: When Hitting Is Still Correct
lessons.content: Do not confuse an uncomfortable hand with a finished hand. Against strong dealer cards, many stiff totals still need to improve.

questions.title: Hard 16 versus dealer 6
questions.scenario: You hold a hard 16 and the dealer shows a 6.
questions.explanation: Hard 16 is awkward, but a dealer 6 is weak enough that standing is better than risking an immediate bust. Do not overplay a bad hand when the dealer is under pressure.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: The instinct is tempting because 16 feels weak, but hitting busts too often and wastes the dealer's bust potential.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: This keeps the dealer under pressure and avoids turning a weak but live hand into a dead one.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: Doubling a fragile total is a common beginner overreach. You would be adding risk in a spot where patience is stronger.

questions.title: Hard 13 versus dealer 2
questions.scenario: You have a hard 13 against a dealer 2.
questions.explanation: A dealer 2 is not powerful enough to force aggression from you. Basic strategy prefers standing because your hand is already ahead of many dealer bust paths.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: The dealer can easily struggle from 2, so your job is often to avoid busting first.

choices.label: Hit once
choices.explanation: Hitting feels proactive, but the mistake is ignoring how often a dealer 2 still fails to complete a strong hand.

choices.label: Surrender
choices.explanation: This hand is not weak enough to give up. Surrender is for much tougher situations.

questions.title: Hard 11 versus dealer 6
questions.scenario: You are dealt a hard 11 and the dealer shows a 6.
questions.explanation: Hard 11 is one of the best doubling hands because you are likely to improve and the dealer is already under pressure with a weak upcard.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: This misunderstands the value of a strong drawing hand. Eleven is not a made hand yet; it is a hand that wants more value.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: You maximize a favorable spot by committing more money when both your draw potential and the dealer's weakness work in your favor.

choices.label: Hit normally
choices.explanation: Hitting is safer than doubling, which is why beginners like it, but it leaves value on the table in a premium spot.

questions.title: Hard 9 versus dealer 3
questions.scenario: You have a hard 9 and the dealer shows a 3.
questions.explanation: Hard 9 can often be doubled against weak dealer cards because many one-card improvements create strong totals while the dealer still starts from a weak position.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting is playable, which makes it tempting, but the mistake is missing an efficient chance to press a favorable spot.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: This uses the growth potential of 9 while the dealer is exposed with a weak upcard.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing on 9 treats a drawing hand like a finished one. You need improvement here, not passivity.

questions.title: Hard 15 versus dealer 10
questions.scenario: You hold a hard 15 and the dealer shows a 10. No surrender is available.
questions.explanation: Without surrender, hard 15 against a dealer 10 is still a hit. The hand is weak, but standing usually loses too often against the dealer's strong starting position.

choices.label: Stand and hope the dealer misses
choices.explanation: The instinct is understandable because 15 is uncomfortable, but the dealer 10 creates too much pressure for passive play to be best.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: You are not happy to hit, but basic strategy prefers trying to improve over surrendering equity by freezing a weak total.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: Doubling compounds a bad position. You have neither the total nor the dealer matchup for that play.

questions.title: Hard 17 versus dealer ace
questions.scenario: You have a hard 17 and the dealer shows an ace.
questions.explanation: Even against a powerful upcard, hard 17 is still a made hand. Hitting would turn a strong non-busting total into an unnecessary bust risk.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Seventeen is usually strong enough to keep, even when the dealer starts from a better position.

choices.label: Hit to try for 20 or 21
choices.explanation: This is a classic greedy mistake. The upside exists, but the bust risk is far too high.

choices.label: Surrender
choices.explanation: Your hand is already too strong to give up half your bet. The problem is the dealer's ace, not your total.

lessons.title: Soft Hands Are Drawing Hands
lessons.content: Soft totals are not just safe hands; they are flexible drawing hands. Many of them gain value by hitting or doubling instead of standing too early.

lessons.title: When Soft 18 Is Not Finished
lessons.content: Soft 18 changes value sharply with the dealer upcard. Sometimes it stands comfortably; other times it still needs action.

lessons.title: Doubling Soft Hands Correctly
lessons.content: Soft doubles work because you can improve aggressively without the usual bust danger. They are strongest against dealer weakness.

lessons.title: When Doubling Builds Edge
lessons.content: Doubling is strongest when you have a hand likely to improve and the dealer is vulnerable. The extra wager only makes sense when the structure of the spot supports it.

lessons.title: Soft Totals Need Pressure
lessons.content: Soft totals look comfortable, but they still need aggressive play in the right windows because they can take another card without busting.

lessons.title: Rule-Sensitive Soft 19 Doubles
lessons.content: Some advanced soft-hand edges only appear when specific rules allow them. Soft 19 versus dealer 6 is a classic case where rule awareness separates a routine stand from a thin value double.

questions.title: Soft 17 versus dealer 3
questions.scenario: You hold A-6 for a soft 17 against a dealer 3.
questions.explanation: Soft 17 is not a finished hand. Against a weak dealer 3, doubling is best because many one-card improvements create useful totals and your ace keeps the bust risk low.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing treats soft 17 like a made hand, but it still needs growth and misses value against dealer weakness.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: This presses a spot where your hand can improve efficiently and safely.

choices.label: Hit only
choices.explanation: Hitting is understandable because it preserves flexibility, but the mistake is not maximizing a favorable doubling opportunity.

questions.title: Soft 17 versus dealer 6
questions.scenario: You hold A-6 and the dealer shows a 6.
questions.explanation: Soft 17 can improve safely, and the dealer 6 is weak enough to make doubling attractive in simplified strategy training.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: You can add pressure while still having room to improve.

choices.label: Stand immediately
choices.explanation: Standing leaves too much value on the table against a weak dealer upcard.

choices.label: Always hit instead
choices.explanation: Hitting is safer than standing, but doubling captures more value in this spot.

questions.title: Soft 18 versus dealer 2
questions.scenario: You have a soft 18 against a dealer 2.
questions.explanation: Against a dealer 2, soft 18 is usually strong enough to stand in standard basic strategy. The dealer is weak enough that you do not need to push a hand that already plays reasonably well.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting feels safe because the hand is soft, but the mistake is over-improving a hand that is already good enough against a weak start.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: This keeps a solid total and lets the weak dealer position create pressure.

choices.label: Split
choices.explanation: There is no pair to split here. This answer confuses soft-hand play with pair play.

questions.title: Best use of soft 18 versus dealer 6
questions.scenario: You hold A-7 against a dealer 6. Which action extracts the most value?
questions.explanation: Soft 18 becomes an aggressive hand against dealer 6. Standard basic strategy favors doubling because the dealer is weak and your hand can still improve without normal bust risk.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing is safe and acceptable in some simplified charts, which makes it tempting, but doubling gains more value in standard basic strategy.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: You combine dealer weakness with a hand that still has efficient upside.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting keeps the hand alive, but it misses the chance to invest more in a premium situation.

questions.title: Soft 15 versus dealer 4
questions.scenario: You have A-4 for a soft 15 against a dealer 4.
questions.explanation: Soft 15 is too weak to stand, but it is an efficient soft double against dealer 4 because your hand has room to grow and the dealer starts in a fragile position.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: This uses both the safety of the soft hand and the dealer's weakness.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing is a passive mistake. Soft 15 is not remotely strong enough to freeze here.

choices.label: Surrender
choices.explanation: You are not in trouble here. This hand is actually a good candidate for controlled aggression.

questions.title: Soft 19 versus dealer 6
questions.scenario: You hold A-8 for a soft 19 against a dealer 6.
questions.explanation: In standard basic strategy, soft 19 is usually a stand, but against dealer 6 it becomes a profitable double in some common rule sets. This assumes standard multi-deck basic strategy where doubling soft 19 versus 6 is allowed.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: This is a nuanced value play. Your hand is already strong, and dealer weakness plus one-card finality can justify pressing the edge.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing is very reasonable and is why many players choose it, but it leaves some value behind under the stated assumption.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting misunderstands the hand completely. Soft 19 is already strong and does not need extra variance.

questions.title: Soft 13 versus dealer 5
questions.scenario: You have A-2 against a dealer 5.
questions.explanation: Soft 13 is weak by total, but flexible by structure. Against a dealer 5, doubling is preferred because many outcomes improve your hand while the dealer is already vulnerable.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting is not terrible, which makes it believable, but the mistake is failing to press a favorable matchup.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: This is a classic soft-hand value spot against dealer weakness.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing on soft 13 massively overvalues the current total. This hand still needs development.

lessons.title: Why Pairs Are Special
lessons.content: A pair is not just a total. Splitting can create two better starting hands, which often matters more than the raw number you currently hold.

lessons.title: Protecting Value With Doubles
lessons.content: Doubling is strongest when your hand has efficient upside and the dealer starts weak. It is not just about having a decent total.

lessons.title: Late Surrender as Damage Control
lessons.content: Surrender is not fear; it is disciplined damage control. Use it only when the hand is so poor that saving half the bet beats playing it out.

lessons.title: Rule-Dependent Pair Exceptions
lessons.content: Advanced pair decisions depend on rules like doubling after split and surrender availability. The correct play can flip once those rules change, so memorize the exception together with the rule that enables it.

questions.title: Pair of 8s versus dealer 10
questions.scenario: You are dealt 8-8 against a dealer 10.
questions.explanation: A pair of 8s is one of the classic splits. Sixteen is a weak total, but splitting gives you two independent chances to build better hands instead of freezing on a bad one.

choices.label: Stand on 16
choices.explanation: Standing is tempting because you want to avoid extra risk, but the mistake is accepting one weak hand instead of creating two playable ones.

choices.label: Split
choices.explanation: This turns a bad total into two new opportunities, which is the core value of splitting 8s.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting 16 is sometimes necessary on non-pair hands, but with 8-8 you have a stronger structural option available.

questions.title: Pair of 10s versus dealer 6
questions.scenario: You have 10-10 against a dealer 6.
questions.explanation: Twenty is already a premium made hand. Splitting is flashy but unnecessary because it breaks a highly profitable total into two weaker starting hands.

choices.label: Split for more upside
choices.explanation: The temptation comes from seeing dealer weakness and wanting two wins, but the mistake is destroying one of your best hands in blackjack.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: You protect a dominant total and let the dealer try to catch up from a weak start.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: You cannot improve a 20 meaningfully enough to justify added risk. The hand is already where you want it.

questions.title: Pair of 9s versus dealer 7
questions.scenario: You are dealt 9-9 against a dealer 7.
questions.explanation: Eighteen is strong, but pair logic still matters. Against dealer 7, splitting 9s is preferred because each 9 can grow into a stronger hand and the dealer's card is not weak enough to make 18 an automatic hold.

choices.label: Stand on 18
choices.explanation: Standing feels natural because 18 is good, but the mistake is ignoring the extra value the pair structure creates in this matchup.

choices.label: Split
choices.explanation: This is one of the important exceptions where the pair is more valuable separated than kept together.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting misunderstands both the strength of 18 and the strategic purpose of pair decisions.

questions.title: Pair of 4s versus dealer 5
questions.scenario: You have 4-4 against a dealer 5. Doubling after split is allowed.
questions.explanation: Under standard basic strategy with doubling after split allowed, 4-4 versus dealer 5 is a split. The goal is not that 8 is strong now; it is that split 4s can become profitable doubled hands.

choices.label: Hit the hard 8
choices.explanation: Hitting is the fallback when split conditions are weaker, so it looks reasonable, but it misses the structural upside created by the stated rule.

choices.label: Stand on 8
choices.explanation: Standing on 8 badly overvalues the current total. This hand still needs development.

choices.label: Split
choices.explanation: This uses the rule environment to create better future doubles and stronger hand growth.

questions.title: Hard 16 versus dealer 9 with late surrender
questions.scenario: You hold a hard 16 against a dealer 9, and late surrender is available.
questions.explanation: With late surrender available, hard 16 versus dealer 9 is a surrender in standard basic strategy. The hand loses often enough that saving half the bet is better than playing it out.

choices.label: Surrender
choices.explanation: This is disciplined damage control in a structurally poor spot.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing is tempting because 16 busts easily, but against dealer 9 the matchup is still bad enough that surrender outperforms it.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: Hitting is the usual fallback without surrender, but once surrender exists, it becomes the cleaner lower-loss option.

questions.title: Hard 15 versus dealer 10 with late surrender
questions.scenario: You have a hard 15 against a dealer 10, and late surrender is allowed.
questions.explanation: Late surrender changes this hand. In standard basic strategy, hard 15 versus dealer 10 is a surrender because the dealer's pressure is high and your hand has limited safe improvement.

choices.label: Hit
choices.explanation: This is the correct backup without surrender, which is why it is tempting, but surrender is stronger once the option exists.

choices.label: Surrender
choices.explanation: You reduce losses in a matchup where playing on is usually worse.

choices.label: Stand
choices.explanation: Standing protects you from busting, but it leaves you too often stuck with a losing total against a powerful dealer start.

questions.title: Hard 11 versus dealer 10
questions.scenario: You have 11 and the dealer shows a 10.
questions.explanation: Hard 11 has strong improvement potential. Even against a strong dealer upcard, doubling keeps the hand aggressive when the math stays favorable.

choices.label: Double down
choices.explanation: You are still favored to improve into a strong total.

choices.label: Stand and hope the dealer busts
choices.explanation: An 11 is too strong a building hand to freeze.

choices.label: Split the hand
choices.explanation: There is nothing to split here.
